tests.title: HANDOFF_NOW — Pattern Activity (polka_dot_patrol)
tests.implemented: Phase 2 integrates select_best_activity into evaluate_handoff; real ActivityDefinition objects flow into _build_handoff_guide.
tests.scenario: Child explored pattern on a ladybug for 4 turns, interest score 75. System selected polka_dot_patrol from catalog.

messages.content: You noticed the ladybug has dots on its back! Those dots are called polka dots.

params.child_answer: Yeah! It has red with black dots!
params.multi_topic_guide:
-SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
-- Default to sight: "What do you notice?", "Which part looks the biggest?", "Is it shiny or dull?"
-- Allow imagination/guessing: "Do you think it would feel rough?", "If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?"
-- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
-- Do NOT use "Do you know…" framing — it creates testing pressure.
-- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ("Let's bark like a puppy!").
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: pattern
Turns explored: 4
Angles already used: (session complete)

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Target attribute: pattern
Activity: Find three polka-dotted things!
Child interest score for this attribute: 75/100
Session turns: 4

HANDOFF MODE: ACTIVE
Your next message must bridge from the current conversation to the activity below.

---

[BRIDGE TO ACTIVITY]
Activity name: Find three polka-dotted things!
Activity description: The child patrols the neighborhood looking for three more spotted things, using the ladybug as the first clue, then turns them into a patrol parade.

Your next message MUST:
1. Mention the activity by name: "Find three polka-dotted things!"
2. Connect it to what you just talked about (one sentence)
3. Ask the child if they want to try it (one short question)
4. End with [ACTIVITY_READY] on its own line

Example of a good bridge:
"You really seem to love colors! Want to try the Color Matching Game? [ACTIVITY_READY]"

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- Asking a deep question about the attribute AFTER mentioning the activity
- Forgetting to say the activity name
- Ending with anything other than [ACTIVITY_READY]
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

checks.criterion: Mentions activity name or preview label
Does NOT ask deep follow-up about pattern after bridging

tests.title: HANDOFF_NOW — Emotion Activity (mood_changer_dog)
tests.implemented: Real bound activity (entity_binding=bound, entity_class_filter=[dog]) selected and injected into handoff prompt.
tests.scenario: Child explored how a stuffed dog feels with interest score 68. System selected mood_changer_dog from catalog.

messages.content: This dog looks so cozy! How do you think it feels right now?

params.multi_topic_guide:
-SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
-- Default to sight: "What do you notice?", "Which part looks the biggest?", "Is it shiny or dull?"
-- Allow imagination/guessing: "Do you think it would feel rough?", "If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?"
-- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
-- Do NOT use "Do you know…" framing — it creates testing pressure.
-- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ("Let's bark like a puppy!").
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: emotion
Turns explored: 4
Angles already used: (session complete)

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Target attribute: emotion
Activity: Translate the dog feelings
Child interest score for this attribute: 68/100
Session turns: 4

HANDOFF MODE: ACTIVE
Your next message must bridge from the current conversation to the activity below.

---

[BRIDGE TO ACTIVITY]
Activity name: Translate the dog feelings
Activity description: The child speaks for a stuffed dog across three situations and names how the dog feels.

Your next message MUST:
1. Mention the activity by name: "Translate the dog feelings"
2. Connect it to what you just talked about (one sentence)
3. Ask the child if they want to try it (one short question)
4. End with [ACTIVITY_READY] on its own line

Example of a good bridge:
"You really seem to love colors! Want to try the Color Matching Game? [ACTIVITY_READY]"

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- Asking a deep question about the attribute AFTER mentioning the activity
- Forgetting to say the activity name
- Ending with anything other than [ACTIVITY_READY]
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

checks.criterion: Mentions activity name or preview label

tests.title: CONTINUE — Observation Angle (Low Interest)
tests.implemented: CARES Phase 0 angle system: select_next_angle unlocks observation only when interest_score < 30.
tests.scenario: Child just started exploring color on an apple, interest score 25. Next angle is observation.

messages.content: Look at this apple! What do you notice about it?

params.multi_topic_guide:
-SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
-- Default to sight: "What do you notice?", "Which part looks the biggest?", "Is it shiny or dull?"
-- Allow imagination/guessing: "Do you think it would feel rough?", "If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?"
-- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
-- Do NOT use "Do you know…" framing — it creates testing pressure.
-- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ("Let's bark like a puppy!").
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: color
Turns explored: 1
Angles already used: (none yet)

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Current attribute: color
Current interest score: 25/100
Session turns: 1
Explored attributes: color

HANDOFF MODE: INACTIVE
Continue exploring the current attribute. Do NOT output [ACTIVITY_READY].

---

[NEXT SUGGESTED ANGLE: observation]
Ask the child to observe and describe the attribute with their own words

For this turn, try using the observation angle:
- Your RESPONSE should: Share one concrete sensory fact about the color
- Your FOLLOW-UP QUESTION should: Ask what the child notices or sees about the color
- Example of a good question: "What color do you see on the apple?"

Already-used angles (try something different if possible):
(none yet)

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- "What color is it?" -- quiz
- "Do you know what color it has?" -- quiz with wrapper
- "What else can you tell me about it?" -- too vague
- "Let us look at its color!" -- forced redirect
- "That is nice, but..." then question about color -- ignoring child
- "Great! Now we can start an activity!" -- mechanical announcement
- Adding [ACTIVITY_READY] after just one shallow exchange -- premature handoff
- Switching topics on a single casual mention -- too sensitive
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

tests.title: CONTINUE — Comparison Angle (Medium Interest)
tests.implemented: Angle selection unlocks comparison when interest_score is 30-55.
tests.scenario: Child explored shape for 2 turns, interest score 42. Next angle is comparison.

messages.content: This apple is round like a ball! What other round things do you know?

params.multi_topic_guide:
-SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
-- Default to sight: "What do you notice?", "Which part looks the biggest?", "Is it shiny or dull?"
-- Allow imagination/guessing: "Do you think it would feel rough?", "If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?"
-- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
-- Do NOT use "Do you know…" framing — it creates testing pressure.
-- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ("Let's bark like a puppy!").
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: shape
Turns explored: 2
Angles already used: observation

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Current attribute: shape
Current interest score: 42/100
Session turns: 2
Explored attributes: shape

HANDOFF MODE: INACTIVE
Continue exploring the current attribute. Do NOT output [ACTIVITY_READY].

---

[NEXT SUGGESTED ANGLE: comparison]
Compare this attribute with something familiar to the child

For this turn, try using the comparison angle:
- Your RESPONSE should: Share a surprising comparison or contrast about the shape
- Your FOLLOW-UP QUESTION should: Ask the child to compare the shape with something they know
- Example of a good question: "Is it more like a banana or a grape in shape?"

Already-used angles (try something different if possible):
- observation: What color do you see on the apple?

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- "What shape is it?" -- quiz
- "Do you know what shape it has?" -- quiz with wrapper
- "What else can you tell me about it?" -- too vague
- "Let us look at its shape!" -- forced redirect
- "That is nice, but..." then question about shape -- ignoring child
- "Great! Now we can start an activity!" -- mechanical announcement
- Adding [ACTIVITY_READY] after just one shallow exchange -- premature handoff
- Switching topics on a single casual mention -- too sensitive
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

tests.title: EXIT_LANE — Session Wrap-up (No Activity)
tests.implemented: CARES EXIT_LANE decision triggers _build_exit_guide when session exceeds MAX_SESSION_TURNS without meeting handoff threshold.
tests.scenario: 8 turns explored across color and shape, best score 35. System triggers EXIT_LANE.

messages.content: The apple is red and round! You know so much about it.

params.multi_topic_guide:
-SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
-- Default to sight: "What do you notice?", "Which part looks the biggest?", "Is it shiny or dull?"
-- Allow imagination/guessing: "Do you think it would feel rough?", "If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?"
-- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
-- Do NOT use "Do you know…" framing — it creates testing pressure.
-- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ("Let's bark like a puppy!").
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: color
Turns explored: 8
Angles already used: (session complete)

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Session turns: 8
Explored attributes: color, shape
Best attribute explored: color
Best interest score: 35/100

EXIT MODE: ACTIVE
The session has been long. Wrap up naturally WITHOUT pushing an activity.

---

[WRAP-UP]
Your next message MUST:
1. Thank the child for exploring with you (one warm sentence)
2. Ask what they want to talk about or explore next (one open-ended question)
3. Keep it light and open — no pressure

Example of a good wrap-up:
"We talked about so many fun things today! What do you want to explore next?"

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- Asking why/how/causal questions about the current attribute
- Pushing an activity or game
- Outputting [ACTIVITY_READY]
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

checks.criterion: Does NOT push activity or game
Does NOT push activity or game (try)

tests.title: REENGAGE — Struggle Recovery (Simple Question)
tests.implemented: CARES REENGAGE decision forces simple angles (observation/comparison only) when child struggles.
tests.scenario: Child said 'I don't know' 3 times about texture. System triggers REENGAGE with observation angle forced.

messages.content: How do you think this dandelion feels?

params.multi_topic_guide:
-SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
-- Default to sight: "What do you notice?", "Which part looks the biggest?", "Is it shiny or dull?"
-- Allow imagination/guessing: "Do you think it would feel rough?", "If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?"
-- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
-- Do NOT use "Do you know…" framing — it creates testing pressure.
-- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ("Let's bark like a puppy!").
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: texture
Turns explored: 3
Angles already used: observation, comparison, preference

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Current attribute: texture
Current interest score: 15/100
Consecutive struggle count: 3

REENGAGE MODE: ACTIVE
The child is struggling. Ask a MUCH simpler, more concrete question.
Use only sensory language (see, look, notice). Single concept only.

---

[NEXT SUGGESTED ANGLE: observation]
Ask the child to observe and describe the attribute with their own words

For this turn, try using the observation angle:
- Your RESPONSE should: Share one concrete sensory fact about the texture
- Your FOLLOW-UP QUESTION should: Ask what the child notices or sees about the texture
- Example of a good question: "What do you notice about the dandelion?"

Already-used angles (try something different if possible):
- observation: What do you notice about the dandelion?
- comparison: Is it more like a banana or a grape in texture?
- preference: Do you like soft dandelions or rough dandelions better?

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- "What texture is it?" -- quiz
- "Do you know what texture it has?" -- quiz with wrapper
- "What else can you tell me about it?" -- too vague
- Open-ended or abstract questions ("what do you think", "why do you think")
- Causal or how/why questions
- Adding [ACTIVITY_READY]
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle
